subtopic_detail: Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis - exchange transfusion indications
stem: A 24-year-old man with known HbSS sickle cell disease presents with severe bilateral lower limb and back pain consistent with a vaso-occlusive crisis. He is being managed with IV morphine patient-controlled analgesia, IV fluids, and supplemental oxygen. On day 2 of admission he develops acute right-sided hemiplegia with expressive aphasia. His baseline Hb is typically 80 to 90 g/L and his current Hb is 72 g/L with HbS fraction 78%.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 148/88 mmHg, RR 20/min, SpO2 96% on 4 L nasal prongs, T 38.2 C
data_table.Investigations: Hb 72 g/L, HbS 78%, reticulocytes 280 x10^9/L, LDH 620 U/L, bilirubin 68 micromol/L, CT brain: no haemorrhage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention for this complication?

options.text: Commence IV alteplase at standard stroke dose given the CT excludes intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: IV thrombolysis is not the standard of care for sickle cell-associated stroke. The mechanism is primarily vaso-occlusive and haemolytic rather than thromboembolic. Exchange transfusion to reduce HbS fraction is the definitive treatment. Thrombolysis carries a high bleeding risk in SCD patients who may have underlying vasculopathy. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

options.text: Arrange urgent automated red cell exchange transfusion targeting HbS fraction below 30%
options.rationale: Acute ischaemic stroke in sickle cell disease is an indication for urgent exchange transfusion (manual or automated apheresis) to reduce HbS fraction below 30% while maintaining Hb below 100 g/L to avoid hyperviscosity. Exchange transfusion is superior to simple transfusion as it reduces HbS rapidly without increasing total Hb. Stroke is the most serious vascular complication of SCD and recurrence risk without chronic transfusion is approximately 70%. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

options.text: Perform simple top-up transfusion to raise Hb to 100 g/L and reassess neurological status
options.rationale: Simple transfusion raises total Hb but does not adequately reduce the HbS fraction. In acute stroke, rapid HbS reduction is essential. Additionally, raising Hb above 100 g/L in SCD risks hyperviscosity and further vaso-occlusion. Exchange transfusion achieves HbS reduction without raising total Hb. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

options.text: Commence therapeutic anticoagulation with IV heparin for presumed large vessel occlusion
options.rationale: SCD-associated stroke has a different pathophysiology from cardioembolic or atherothrombotic stroke. The primary mechanism involves sickling-mediated endothelial damage and large vessel vasculopathy. Exchange transfusion to reduce HbS is the treatment of choice, not anticoagulation. Anticoagulation may be considered adjunctively in some cases but is not first-line. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone to reduce cerebral oedema and refer for urgent thrombectomy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are associated with rebound vaso-occlusive events in SCD and are generally avoided. Thrombectomy is not established for SCD-associated stroke, which involves a different vasculopathy from standard large vessel occlusion. The priority is exchange transfusion. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

explanation.summary: Stroke occurs in approximately 11% of HbSS patients by age 20 and is one of the most devastating complications of sickle cell disease. The mechanism involves sickling-mediated endothelial injury, large vessel vasculopathy, and intimal hyperplasia rather than conventional atherothrombosis. Emergency exchange transfusion to reduce HbS below 30% is the standard of care. After an initial stroke, chronic transfusion therapy to maintain HbS below 30% is required to prevent recurrence, which otherwise approaches 70% within 3 years.
key_points: Acute stroke in SCD requires urgent exchange transfusion targeting HbS below 30%
Simple transfusion is inadequate as it does not sufficiently reduce HbS fraction
Total Hb should not exceed 100 g/L post-transfusion to avoid hyperviscosity
IV thrombolysis is not standard of care for SCD-associated stroke
Chronic transfusion therapy is required after initial stroke to prevent recurrence
explanation.pearls: Transcranial Doppler velocities above 200 cm/s in the internal carotid or middle cerebral artery identify children with SCD at high stroke risk - the STOP trial showed chronic transfusion reduces first stroke incidence by 92% in this population.

subtopic_detail: Acute splenic sequestration crisis in adult HbSC disease
stem: A 22-year-old woman with known HbSC disease presents with sudden-onset left upper quadrant pain, nausea, and light-headedness. She has had no prior splenic complications. Her baseline Hb is typically 110 g/L. On examination she appears pale and diaphoretic. Her spleen is palpable 6 cm below the left costal margin, which is new from her last clinic visit 3 months ago where the spleen was not palpable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128 bpm, BP 82/52 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air, T 37.6 C
data_table.Investigations: Hb 52 g/L (baseline 110), reticulocytes 420 x10^9/L, platelets 68 x10^9/L (baseline 220), LDH 890 U/L, bilirubin 82 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Splenic infarction with haemolytic crisis - commence IV fluids and simple red cell transfusion
options.rationale: Splenic infarction causes pain but does not typically cause the acute rapid splenic enlargement, profound anaemia with Hb drop of 58 g/L, and thrombocytopaenia seen here. The rapidly enlarging spleen with acute anaemia is characteristic of splenic sequestration, not simple infarction. Simple transfusion must be given cautiously in sequestration to avoid hyperviscosity when red cells are released from the spleen. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

options.text: Hypersplenism from chronic congestion - arrange elective splenectomy within the coming weeks
options.rationale: Hypersplenism develops gradually with chronic splenomegaly and mild cytopaenias. This presentation is acute with rapid-onset splenomegaly, profound anaemia, and haemodynamic instability consistent with acute sequestration. Elective management is inappropriate when the patient is in hypovolaemic shock. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines)

options.text: Acute splenic sequestration - commence cautious transfusion targeting Hb 80 to 90 g/L and plan splenectomy
options.rationale: Acute splenic sequestration occurs when sickled red cells trap rapidly in the spleen, causing acute splenomegaly, profound anaemia (Hb drop of greater than 20 g/L below baseline), thrombocytopaenia, and hypovolaemic shock. It is more common in HbSC disease adults because the spleen may not have auto-infarcted as in HbSS. Cautious transfusion is critical - target Hb 80 to 90 g/L only, as auto-transfusion from the spleen when sequestration resolves can cause dangerous Hb overshoot and hyperviscosity. Splenectomy is recommended after a single sequestration episode in adults. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

options.text: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 - check parvovirus serology and transfuse to baseline Hb
options.rationale: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 causes profound anaemia with inappropriately low reticulocytes due to erythroid precursor destruction. This patient has markedly elevated reticulocytes at 420 x10^9/L, indicating active marrow response, which excludes aplastic crisis. The acute splenomegaly and thrombocytopaenia point to sequestration. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines)

options.text: Haemolytic crisis with DIC - commence exchange transfusion and IV heparin for coagulopathy
options.rationale: While haemolysis markers are elevated, the thrombocytopaenia here reflects platelet trapping in the congested spleen, not DIC. DIC would show prolonged PT/APTT and elevated D-dimer with fibrinogen consumption. The acute splenomegaly with Hb drop and thrombocytopaenia is pathognomonic for splenic sequestration. Heparin in acute SCD crisis would increase bleeding risk. (Source: BSH Sickle Cell Guidelines; HSANZ)

explanation.summary: Acute splenic sequestration is a life-threatening emergency in sickle cell disease where sickled red cells rapidly pool in the spleen, causing acute splenomegaly, profound anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, and hypovolaemic shock. While it most commonly affects young children with HbSS, adults with HbSC disease or HbS-beta-thalassaemia are at risk because their spleens often remain functional. A key management principle is cautious transfusion to Hb 80 to 90 g/L only, because when the sequestration episode resolves, trapped cells are released back into circulation, causing dangerous Hb overshoot. Splenectomy is recommended after a single episode in adults.
key_points: Acute splenic sequestration presents with rapid splenomegaly, profound anaemia, and thrombocytopaenia
HbSC patients retain splenic function into adulthood unlike HbSS patients
Transfusion must be cautious to avoid Hb overshoot when sequestered cells are released
Target Hb of 80 to 90 g/L only during the acute episode
Splenectomy is recommended after a single episode in adults to prevent recurrence
explanation.pearls: HbSC patients retain splenic function into adulthood because HbC polymerises less aggressively than HbS, resulting in less splenic infarction - this paradoxically makes them susceptible to acute splenic sequestration at ages where HbSS patients are already functionally asplenic.

subtopic_detail: ABG interpretation - mixed acid-base disorder identification
stem: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and COPD presents with 3 days of vomiting, polyuria, and increasing confusion. He has been unable to take his metformin or fluids for 3 days. He appears dehydrated with Kussmaul breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 96/62 mmHg, RR 32/min deep, SpO2 94% on room air, T 37.2 C
data_table.Investigations: ABG: pH 7.18, pCO2 22 mmHg, pO2 92 mmHg, HCO3 8 mmol/L, BE -18. Na 138 mmol/L, K 5.8 mmol/L, Cl 98 mmol/L, BSL 38.2 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, Cr 248 micromol/L, urea 22 mmol/L

options.text: Simple high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation only
options.rationale: Anion gap is 138 - (98 + 8) = 32, confirming a high anion gap metabolic acidosis from DKA. Winter formula predicts expected pCO2 = 1.5 x 8 + 8 = 20 mmHg. The actual pCO2 of 22 mmHg is close but slightly higher. The delta-delta ratio (change in AG / change in HCO3) = (32-12) / (24-8) = 20/16 = 1.25, which is between 1 and 2, suggesting a pure HAGMA. However, the elevated urea and creatinine suggest a component of pre-renal AKI contributing additional unmeasured anions. (Source: eTG: Respiratory; BMJ Best Practice: DKA)

options.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation
options.rationale: The pCO2 of 22 mmHg is appropriate compensation for an HCO3 of 8 mmol/L per Winter formula (expected 20 plus or minus 2 mmHg). This is compensatory Kussmaul breathing, not a primary respiratory alkalosis. A primary respiratory alkalosis would be suggested by pCO2 significantly lower than predicted. (Source: eTG: Respiratory)

options.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal impairment
options.rationale: A concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis would be suggested by a delta-delta ratio below 1 (delta AG less than delta HCO3). Here the delta-delta is 1.25, which is between 1 and 2, consistent with a pure HAGMA. While renal impairment is present, it is contributing to the HAGMA (uraemic acidosis) rather than causing a separate NAGMA. (Source: eTG: Respiratory)

options.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis suggested by the delta-delta ratio
options.rationale: The anion gap is 32 (elevated by 20 above normal of 12). The HCO3 is 8 (decreased by 16 below normal of 24). The delta-delta ratio is 20/16 = 1.25. Strictly, a ratio between 1 and 2 is consistent with a pure HAGMA. However, in this clinical context with 3 days of vomiting (which causes metabolic alkalosis from HCl loss), the HCO3 should be even lower if the HAGMA were the only process. The delta AG exceeding the delta HCO3 (ratio above 1) in a vomiting patient suggests a concurrent metabolic alkalosis is partially offsetting the acidosis, raising the HCO3 above what pure DKA alone would produce. (Source: eTG: Respiratory; BMJ Best Practice: ABG Interpretation)

options.text: Mixed respiratory acidosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis from COPD and diabetic ketoacidosis
options.rationale: A respiratory acidosis would be indicated by an elevated pCO2. The pCO2 of 22 mmHg is low, reflecting appropriate respiratory compensation for the metabolic acidosis, not respiratory acidosis. Despite having COPD, this patient is able to mount an appropriate ventilatory response. Respiratory acidosis would be concerning if pCO2 were higher than predicted. (Source: eTG: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Systematic ABG interpretation requires a stepwise approach: identify the primary disturbance from pH, assess compensation, calculate the anion gap, and apply the delta-delta ratio to identify mixed disorders. In this case, the primary disturbance is a high anion gap metabolic acidosis from DKA with uraemic contribution. The delta-delta ratio of 1.25, combined with the clinical history of prolonged vomiting, suggests a concurrent metabolic alkalosis that is partially masking the severity of the acidosis. Without the vomiting-induced alkalosis, the HCO3 would be even lower.
key_points: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3); normal approximately 12 in Australia
Delta-delta ratio = change in AG / change in HCO3; values above 1 may indicate concurrent metabolic alkalosis
Winter formula predicts expected pCO2 for respiratory compensation in metabolic acidosis
Clinical context (vomiting) is essential for interpreting the delta-delta ratio
DKA and uraemia both contribute unmeasured anions to a high anion gap
explanation.pearls: In DKA with prolonged vomiting, the delta-delta ratio consistently exceeds 1.0 because gastric HCl loss generates a metabolic alkalosis that partially buffers the HCO3 fall - this hidden alkalosis means the HCO3 underestimates the true severity of ketoacid production.

subtopic_detail: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with osmolal gap - toxic alcohol
stem: A 45-year-old man is found confused in a park and brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He smells of alcohol. He is unable to provide a history. On examination he has nystagmus, ataxia, and appears intoxicated but there is no focal neurological deficit. His serum ethanol level returns as undetectable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 98 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 97% on room air, T 36.4 C
data_table.Investigations: ABG: pH 7.12, pCO2 18 mmHg, HCO3 6 mmol/L. Na 140 mmol/L, K 4.8 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, BSL 5.4 mmol/L, ketones 0.4 mmol/L, Cr 188 micromol/L, measured serum osmolality 326 mOsm/kg, calculated osmolality 289 mOsm/kg, urine microscopy: envelope-shaped crystals

lead_in: What is the most likely cause and the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Methanol poisoning - commence IV ethanol infusion and arrange urgent haemodialysis
options.rationale: Methanol poisoning causes a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with osmolal gap but does NOT produce calcium oxalate crystals in urine. Methanol is metabolised to formic acid, which causes optic nerve toxicity. The envelope-shaped crystals (calcium oxalate monohydrate/dihydrate) are pathognomonic for ethylene glycol, not methanol. (Source: eTG: Toxicology; ANZCA Toxicology Guidelines)

options.text: Isopropanol ingestion - provide supportive care as this does not cause significant acidosis
options.rationale: Isopropanol is metabolised to acetone, which causes an osmolal gap and ketonaemia but NOT a significant anion gap metabolic acidosis. This patient has a severe HAGMA with pH 7.12 and HCO3 6 mmol/L, which excludes isopropanol as the sole ingestion. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: Starvation ketoacidosis - administer IV dextrose and thiamine and reassess in 4 hours
options.rationale: Starvation ketoacidosis produces elevated ketones and a HAGMA but would not cause an osmolal gap of 37 mOsm/kg or calcium oxalate crystalluria. The ketones here are only 0.4 mmol/L, which is normal. The osmolal gap and crystal pattern are diagnostic of toxic alcohol. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: Ethylene glycol poisoning - administer IV fomepizole and arrange urgent haemodialysis
options.rationale: This is almost the correct answer, and IV fomepizole and haemodialysis are indeed the mainstay of treatment. However, the option text describes administering fomepizole alone before dialysis, whereas this patient meets criteria for immediate dialysis (pH below 7.15, renal failure, severe metabolic acidosis). Both must be commenced simultaneously. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: Ethylene glycol poisoning - commence IV fomepizole and simultaneous urgent haemodialysis
options.rationale: The combination of severe HAGMA (anion gap 32), large osmolal gap (326 - 289 = 37 mOsm/kg), undetectable ethanol, calcium oxalate crystalluria, and AKI is diagnostic of ethylene glycol poisoning. Fomepizole (15 mg/kg IV loading) inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase, blocking conversion to glycolic and oxalic acids. Haemodialysis removes both the parent compound and toxic metabolites. Indications for dialysis include pH below 7.3, renal failure, or ethylene glycol level above 50 mg/dL. This patient meets multiple criteria. (Source: eTG: Toxicology; Toxicology Handbook)

explanation.summary: Ethylene glycol poisoning presents with an apparent intoxication (CNS depression, ataxia, nystagmus) that progresses through three stages: neurological, cardiopulmonary, and renal. The diagnostic hallmarks are severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis, elevated osmolal gap, AKI, and calcium oxalate crystalluria on urine microscopy. Envelope-shaped crystals indicate calcium oxalate dihydrate. Treatment involves fomepizole to block alcohol dehydrogenase and urgent haemodialysis when pH is below 7.3, renal failure is present, or measured levels exceed 50 mg/dL.
key_points: Ethylene glycol causes HAGMA plus osmolal gap plus calcium oxalate crystalluria
Methanol causes HAGMA plus osmolal gap but no oxalate crystals and causes optic toxicity
Isopropanol causes osmolal gap and ketonaemia without significant HAGMA
Fomepizole 15 mg/kg IV loading dose blocks alcohol dehydrogenase
Haemodialysis indications include pH below 7.3, renal failure, or level above 50 mg/dL
explanation.pearls: The osmolal gap may normalise despite worsening acidosis in late ethylene glycol poisoning because the parent compound has been fully metabolised to glycolic and oxalic acid - a normal osmolal gap does NOT exclude toxic alcohol ingestion if the presentation is delayed.

subtopic_detail: Lung cancer screening eligibility and LDCT referral
stem: A 58-year-old man presents for a routine health assessment. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and quit smoking 8 years ago. He has no respiratory symptoms. He asks about lung cancer screening after seeing a media report about the Australian lung cancer screening program.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72 bpm, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Normal respiratory examination, no clubbing, no lymphadenopathy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate response regarding lung cancer screening eligibility?

options.text: He is eligible for biennial LDCT screening under the National Lung Cancer Screening Program
options.rationale: The Australian National Lung Cancer Screening Program (commenced 2025) offers biennial low-dose CT to adults aged 50 to 70 years with a 30 or more pack-year smoking history who are current smokers or have quit within the past 15 years. This patient meets all criteria: aged 58, 40-pack-year history, quit 8 years ago. LDCT screening reduces lung cancer mortality by approximately 20% based on the NELSON and NLST trials. (Source: Australian Government NLCSP; Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: He is ineligible because he quit smoking more than 5 years ago and his risk has normalised
options.rationale: Lung cancer risk remains substantially elevated for at least 15 years after smoking cessation. The Australian NLCSP eligibility extends to former smokers who quit within the past 15 years. Having quit 8 years ago, this patient remains within the eligible window. (Source: Australian Government NLCSP)

options.text: He should have an annual chest X-ray as the recommended initial lung cancer screening modality
options.rationale: Chest X-ray is not an effective lung cancer screening tool. Multiple randomised trials have shown no mortality reduction from chest X-ray screening. LDCT is the only imaging modality with proven mortality benefit for lung cancer screening. (Source: Cancer Council Australia; NLST)

options.text: He should undergo sputum cytology combined with spirometry as the recommended screening pathway
options.rationale: Sputum cytology has poor sensitivity for early-stage peripheral lung cancers and is not recommended for screening. Spirometry assesses airway function but does not detect lung cancer. Neither is part of the evidence-based screening pathway. (Source: Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: He is ineligible because screening is only offered to current smokers with active respiratory symptoms
options.rationale: The NLCSP does not require current smoking status or respiratory symptoms. Screening targets asymptomatic high-risk individuals to detect early-stage disease before symptoms develop. Both current and recent former smokers meeting age and pack-year criteria are eligible. (Source: Australian Government NLCSP)

explanation.summary: Australia launched the National Lung Cancer Screening Program in 2025, offering biennial LDCT to high-risk individuals aged 50 to 70 with a 30 or more pack-year smoking history who are current smokers or quit within 15 years. This follows evidence from the NLST and NELSON trials showing 20 to 24% lung cancer mortality reduction with LDCT screening. The program includes shared decision-making, smoking cessation support, and structured reporting with standardised nodule management pathways.
key_points: LDCT is the only evidence-based lung cancer screening modality
Australian NLCSP eligibility: age 50 to 70, 30+ pack-years, current or quit within 15 years
Screening is biennial under the Australian program
LDCT screening reduces lung cancer mortality by approximately 20 to 24%
Chest X-ray and sputum cytology are not effective for lung cancer screening
explanation.pearls: The NELSON trial showed LDCT screening reduced lung cancer mortality by 24% in men and up to 33% in women over 10 years - the Australian program adopted biennial rather than annual screening based on modelling showing comparable outcomes with lower cumulative radiation exposure and false positive rates.

subtopic_detail: Incidental lung mass urgent referral and staging workup
stem: A 64-year-old woman who is a current smoker (45 pack-years) presents with a 6-week history of persistent cough, haemoptysis, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg. A chest X-ray shows a 3.5 cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe with ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. She has no prior imaging for comparison.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82 bpm, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Investigations: CXR: 3.5 cm spiculated RUL mass with right hilar lymphadenopathy, FBC and UEC normal, calcium 2.82 mmol/L (corrected), LDH 310 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in the investigation pathway?

options.text: Refer for CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung mass before any further imaging
options.rationale: While tissue diagnosis is essential, staging with contrast-enhanced CT chest and upper abdomen is performed before biopsy to characterise the mass, assess mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and identify distant metastases. Biopsy without staging may lead to suboptimal sampling or delay the complete workup. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines; eTG: Respiratory)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Arrange urgent contrast CT chest and upper abdomen and refer to a multidisciplinary lung cancer team
options.rationale: A spiculated mass with hilar lymphadenopathy in a heavy smoker with haemoptysis is highly suspicious for lung cancer. The immediate priority is contrast-enhanced CT of chest and upper abdomen (to include liver and adrenals) for staging, followed by urgent referral to a multidisciplinary team. Australian guidelines recommend CT within 2 weeks and specialist review within 4 weeks of suspected lung cancer. The hypercalcaemia may indicate squamous cell carcinoma secreting PTHrP. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines; eTG: Respiratory)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Commence empiric antibiotics for 6 weeks to exclude an infective cause before further workup
options.rationale: A 6-week trial of antibiotics is inappropriate when the clinical and radiological features are highly suspicious for malignancy. Spiculated morphology, hilar lymphadenopathy, haemoptysis, weight loss, and heavy smoking history demand urgent cancer pathway investigation. Delaying workup for an empiric antibiotic trial risks avoidable disease progression. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Arrange PET-CT as the first investigation to determine metabolic activity and distant metastases
options.rationale: PET-CT is a staging investigation that follows initial CT characterisation. It is used to assess mediastinal nodal involvement and distant metastases once CT has defined the primary lesion. It should not replace contrast CT as the first-line investigation. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines; eTG: Respiratory)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Repeat the chest X-ray in 6 weeks to assess for interval growth before proceeding with CT
options.rationale: Serial CXR monitoring is appropriate for small indeterminate pulmonary nodules but not for a 3.5 cm spiculated mass with hilar lymphadenopathy and red-flag symptoms. This requires immediate escalation through the urgent cancer pathway without delay. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

explanation.summary: Suspected lung cancer requires urgent investigation through a streamlined pathway. Australian guidelines recommend contrast-enhanced CT chest and upper abdomen within 2 weeks of clinical suspicion, followed by MDT review within 4 weeks. PET-CT follows CT for staging of potentially curable disease. Tissue diagnosis is obtained via the least invasive method appropriate to the lesion location. Hypercalcaemia in the setting of lung cancer suggests squamous cell carcinoma producing PTH-related peptide. All patients should be discussed at a lung cancer multidisciplinary meeting before treatment decisions.
key_points: Contrast CT chest and upper abdomen is the first staging investigation in suspected lung cancer
Australian guidelines target CT within 2 weeks and specialist review within 4 weeks
PET-CT follows CT for staging of potentially resectable disease
Hypercalcaemia suggests squamous cell carcinoma producing PTHrP
MDT discussion before treatment is standard of care in Australia
explanation.pearls: Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for approximately 25% of lung cancers and is the subtype most commonly associated with humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy via PTHrP secretion - corrected calcium above 2.6 mmol/L in a smoker with a lung mass should prompt urgent investigation.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Lung Cancer Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cardiovascular risk assessment
stem: A 38-year-old Aboriginal man presents to his Aboriginal Medical Service for a routine health check. He has no known medical conditions and takes no medications. He smokes 15 cigarettes per day. His father died of a myocardial infarction at age 42. He works as a mechanic and has a sedentary lifestyle outside work.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 138/88 mmHg, BMI 31 kg/m2
data_table.Investigations: Total cholesterol 5.8 mmol/L, LDL 3.6 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.4 mmol/L, BSL fasting 5.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.9%, eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73m2, ACR 4.2 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate approach to cardiovascular risk assessment in this patient?

options.text: Apply the standard Framingham risk equation and treat only if the 10-year risk exceeds 15%
options.rationale: The Framingham risk equation was not validated in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations and significantly underestimates cardiovascular risk in this group. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples experience cardiovascular disease at younger ages and at higher rates. Australian guidelines recommend using the modified Australian absolute cardiovascular risk calculator with specific adjustments. (Source: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health; NHFA/CSANZ)
source_refs: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health

options.text: Defer formal risk assessment until age 45 as recommended for the general male population
options.rationale: Cardiovascular risk assessment should begin at age 18 for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, compared with age 45 for non-Indigenous men. This reflects the earlier onset and higher burden of cardiovascular disease in this population. Deferring to age 45 would miss decades of modifiable risk. (Source: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health; NHFA/CSANZ)
source_refs: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health

options.text: Classify as high cardiovascular risk given Aboriginal status with multiple risk factors and treat accordingly
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recognise that Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples aged 30 and over with any modifiable risk factor should be reclassified upward from their calculated risk. This patient has multiple risk factors: smoking, family history of premature IHD, hypertension (138/88), dyslipidaemia (high LDL, low HDL, high TG), BMI 31, and microalbuminuria. He should be classified as high risk and commenced on antihypertensive therapy, statin, and smoking cessation support. (Source: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health; NHFA/CSANZ)
source_refs: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health

options.text: Calculate his absolute risk using the standard Australian calculator and treat based on that result only
options.rationale: The standard Australian absolute cardiovascular risk calculator underestimates risk in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Guidelines recommend reclassification upward for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adults aged 30 and over with any modifiable risk factor, rather than relying solely on calculator output. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Cardiovascular Risk Guidelines)

options.text: Focus exclusively on lifestyle modification for 12 months before considering pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: While lifestyle modification is essential, delaying pharmacotherapy for 12 months in a high-risk patient with multiple risk factors is inappropriate. Concurrent commencement of lifestyle changes (smoking cessation, diet, exercise) and pharmacotherapy (statin, antihypertensive) is recommended for high-risk patients. (Source: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health; NHFA/CSANZ)
source_refs: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health

explanation.summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples experience cardiovascular disease at rates three times higher than the general Australian population, with onset 10 to 20 years earlier. Australian guidelines recommend cardiovascular risk assessment from age 18 (compared to 45 for non-Indigenous men) and upward reclassification for those aged 30 and over with any modifiable risk factor. Management combines culturally appropriate lifestyle interventions with prompt pharmacotherapy for high-risk individuals. The Aboriginal Medical Service model provides integrated, culturally safe primary care that improves engagement with chronic disease management.
key_points: CVD risk assessment begins at age 18 for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples
Reclassify risk upward for those aged 30 and over with any modifiable risk factor
Standard risk calculators underestimate cardiovascular risk in this population
Multiple risk factors at a young age warrant classification as high risk with prompt treatment
Culturally safe care through Aboriginal Medical Services improves chronic disease outcomes
explanation.pearls: Microalbuminuria (ACR 2.5 to 25 mg/mmol in males) is both an independent cardiovascular risk marker and an early indicator of renal disease in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples - it should be checked annually from age 18 as part of the adult health check (MBS item 715).

sources.label: RACGP National Guide to Preventive Health

subtopic_detail: Rheumatic heart disease screening and secondary prophylaxis in Aboriginal communities
stem: A 14-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote Northern Territory community presents with her third episode of acute rheumatic fever. She was diagnosed with her first episode at age 8 and has been on secondary prophylaxis with intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin but has missed several doses due to difficulties accessing the clinic. Echocardiography performed today shows moderate mitral regurgitation with mild mitral stenosis. Her cardiac function is preserved.

data_table.Investigations: Echocardiography: moderate MR with mild MS, LA mildly dilated, LVEF 58%, no aortic valve involvement, ESR 48 mm/hr, ASO titre 640 IU/mL (elevated)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate duration of secondary prophylaxis for this patient?

options.text: Continue IM benzathine benzylpenicillin every 4 weeks until age 21 then cease prophylaxis entirely
options.rationale: Age 21 is the minimum duration for ARF without carditis. This patient has established rheumatic heart disease with moderate mitral regurgitation and recurrent ARF episodes. Australian RHD guidelines recommend longer prophylaxis duration for patients with persistent valvular disease. (Source: RHDAustralia ARF/RHD Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg twice daily as it is easier to adhere to than injections
options.rationale: Oral penicillin has significantly lower adherence and higher ARF recurrence rates than IM benzathine benzylpenicillin. For patients with established RHD, intramuscular prophylaxis is strongly recommended. In remote settings, adherence to oral medication is especially problematic. The issue of missed doses should be addressed through outreach strategies, not by switching to a less effective route. (Source: RHDAustralia ARF/RHD Guidelines)

options.text: Cease prophylaxis as three episodes of ARF indicate treatment failure and surgery is now required
options.rationale: Recurrent ARF does not indicate prophylaxis failure if doses have been missed. Rather, the recurrences confirm the importance of consistent prophylaxis. Surgery is reserved for severe symptomatic valvular disease, not moderate MR with preserved cardiac function. Continued prophylaxis prevents further valve damage. (Source: RHDAustralia ARF/RHD Guidelines)

options.text: Continue IM benzathine benzylpenicillin every 4 weeks until age 40 or for 10 years after last ARF episode
options.rationale: Australian RHDAustralia guidelines recommend secondary prophylaxis for rheumatic heart disease with persistent moderate or severe valvular disease until age 40, or for a minimum of 10 years after the last episode of ARF, whichever is longer. This patient has moderate MR with recurrent ARF. Addressing the missed doses through outreach nursing, reminder systems, and community engagement is essential. Every 4-week dosing is preferred over every 3-week in the Australian context. (Source: RHDAustralia ARF/RHD Guidelines)

options.text: Continue prophylaxis for life given the severity of her rheumatic heart disease and recurrence pattern
options.rationale: Lifelong prophylaxis may be considered for severe RHD (mechanical valve replacement, previous cardiac surgery) but is not the standard recommendation for moderate valvular disease with preserved function. The guideline recommendation is until age 40 or 10 years after the last ARF episode. Lifelong prophylaxis may be considered individually but is not first-line for moderate disease. (Source: RHDAustralia ARF/RHD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute rheumatic fever and rheumatic heart disease remain significant causes of morbidity and mortality in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, particularly in remote and tropical regions. Secondary prophylaxis with intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin is the cornerstone of RHD management, preventing recurrent ARF episodes that cause progressive valve damage. Duration depends on severity: 5 years or until age 21 for ARF without carditis, 10 years or until age 21 for ARF with carditis but no RHD, and until age 40 for persistent moderate or severe RHD. Adherence support through register-based recall systems and outreach nursing is critical.
key_points: IM benzathine benzylpenicillin every 4 weeks is the Australian standard for secondary prophylaxis
Prophylaxis duration varies by disease severity: age 21, age 35, or age 40 depending on valve involvement
Moderate or severe RHD requires prophylaxis until age 40 or 10 years after last ARF
Oral penicillin has lower adherence and higher recurrence rates than IM prophylaxis
RHD register-based recall systems improve adherence in remote communities
explanation.pearls: Australia has one of the highest rates of rheumatic heart disease in the developed world, concentrated almost entirely in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities in northern and central Australia - the age-standardised RHD prevalence in these populations is approximately 150 times that of non-Indigenous Australians.
